title: Morse Theory of Loop Spaces and Hecke Algebras
speaker: Roman Krutowski
abstract: One can associate an HDHF (symmetric) wrapped Fukaya category to a Liouville domain by counting higher genus curves, which are required to be branched covers. For the cotangent bundle of an orientable surface with genus at least one Honda, Tian, and Yuan showed that the A∞-algebra associated with k disjoint cotangent fibers is quasi-equivalent to the HOMFLY-PT braid skein algebra of the surface. In this talk, I will present a Morse-theoretic model that computes the HDHF A∞-algebra of k fibers of the cotangent bundle of an orientable smooth manifold. We use this model to describe the A∞-algebra of k cotangent fibers of the two-dimensional sphere, and show that it is quasi-equivalent to a certain dga. This talk is based on a joint work with Honda, Tian, and Yuan.

proof_stages.label: Quadratic Lagrangian path-space Morse theory
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the action functional on W^{1,2} path and loop spaces for fiberwise convex quadratic Lagrangians and recalls that suitable smooth Morse--Smale pseudo-gradients exist, with smooth unstable manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Morse-theoretic string operations
proof_stages.summary: Concatenation of paths and Morse flow trees are used to define product and A-infinity string-topology operations, with perturbations and gluing explaining the A-infinity relations.

proof_stages.label: Multipath spaces and switching maps
proof_stages.summary: For κ-tuples of endpoints the speaker defines disconnected multipath spaces, collision diagonals with time parameters, and reparametrized switching maps that let a Morse trajectory jump between permutation components.

proof_stages.label: Switched Morse A-infinity algebra
proof_stages.summary: Moduli spaces of Morse flow lines and trees with switch markers are made transverse and compactified, yielding an hbar-completed differential and A-infinity operations; the later Q&A notes the compatible action filtration.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic target for the S^2 computation
proof_stages.summary: After recalling braid surface Hecke algebras and prior surface results, the speaker states the S^2 answer as a DGA with braid generators, one degree -1 generator, specified relations and differential, and discusses a conjectural derived skein interpretation.

proof_stages.label: Sphere computation and disappearance of higher operations
proof_stages.summary: Key relations and the differential are checked by explicit flow-and-resolution counts, and Katok Finsler metrics together with continuation/two-color-tree quasi-equivalences give a direct-limit DGA in which higher A-infinity operations vanish.

proof_stages.label: Floer model with branched covers
proof_stages.summary: On the cotangent bundle side, the speaker defines an A-infinity algebra for κ cotangent fibers by counting Floer curves over κ-fold simply branched covers of punctured disks, where branch points play the role of Morse switches.

proof_stages.label: Morse--Floer quasi-equivalence
proof_stages.summary: A quasi-equivalence between the branched-cover Floer algebra and the switched Morse algebra is constructed via hybrid holomorphic-cover/Morse-switch buildings, with hyperbolic boundary-node gluing and the hbar=0 Viterbo-type comparison establishing quasi-isomorphism.

examples.name: Kinetic-energy Lagrangian and potential perturbations
examples.note: The half-norm-squared Lagrangian, optionally plus a base-dependent potential, makes the fiberwise quadratic hypotheses concrete but is not used to derive the general construction.
examples.engagement_reason: The half-norm-squared Lagrangian and potential perturbations are given as standard examples, with only the fact stated that the former yields geodesics.

passes.reason: Standard kinetic-plus-potential Lagrangians are representative fiberwise-quadratic convex examples, not a failure case.

examples.name: Two-strand multipath components
examples.note: The case κ=2 illustrates that the multipath space has identity and transposition components, clarifying why switching maps can move between components.
examples.engagement_reason: For the two-strand case the speaker stacks two t1 strands, follows the flow, identifies the crossing, applies the switch/resolution, and derives the terms in t1^2 = 1 + hbar t1.

passes.reason: The two-strand case is the minimal nontrivial multipath/switching setup with only identity and transposition components.

examples.name: Punctured torus surface Hecke algebra as DAHA
examples.note: Morton--Samuelson's torus case is cited to show that braid surface Hecke algebras recover a familiar double affine Hecke algebra, giving context rather than a proof ingredient for the sphere computation.
examples.engagement_reason: The punctured-torus surface Hecke algebra is cited as known to be isomorphic to Cherednik’s DAHA, without any computation or verification.

passes.reason: The punctured-torus surface Hecke algebra is cited as a systematic known identification with DAHA, not as a sporadic or adversarial object.

examples.name: Flat-torus based geodesics
examples.note: The flat torus/based-path comparison illustrates why earlier positive-genus computations reduce to an ordinary algebra, contrasting with the many-index geodesics on S^2.
examples.engagement_reason: Flat-torus based geodesics are only described as unique in a fixed homology class with fixed endpoints.

passes.reason: A flat torus is a highly regular locally symmetric metric example whose based geodesics are unique/index-zero in homology classes.

examples.name: Zermelo wind picture for Katok metrics
examples.note: The wind-on-the-sphere interpretation explains the asymmetry between long and short equatorial geodesics in the Finsler metric, but the proof uses the resulting action estimates rather than the analogy itself.
examples.engagement_reason: The Katok/Zermelo wind metric is used concretely to compare equatorial geodesic directions, compute their lengths, and infer action-index linearity for vanishing arguments.

passes.reason: Irrational Katok/Zermelo Randers metrics are highly regular constant-curvature Finsler examples that are dynamically extremal in their closed-geodesic behavior, here used constructively.
irrational Katok-Zermelo metrics are sharp finite-closed-geodesic Finsler examples used here to separate actions
Irrational Katok/Zermelo Randers metrics are extremal Finsler-sphere examples with minimal closed-geodesic dynamics, used here to separate actions.
Katok/Zermelo wind metrics are used in a limiting near-maximal-wind regime to force extreme action-index separation, not as counterexamples.
Katok/Zermelo Finsler spheres are special metrics realizing the finite/minimal closed-geodesic extreme, used as a computational device
The Katok/Zermelo metrics use a wind parameter near the allowable boundary to create extremal length/action separation.
Katok/Zermelo metrics form an infinite extremal Finsler family famous for minimal closed-geodesic behavior, here used as a computational device.
Irrational Katok/Zermelo-wind metrics are dynamically extremal Finsler examples with very few closed geodesics, used here as a computational simplification.
irrational Katok/Zermelo Finsler spheres are sharp highly structured examples with minimal closed-geodesic behavior
Katok/Zermelo metrics form a special near-boundary Randers family used to create extreme action-length separation, not to disprove a claim.
Katok/Zermelo metrics are used as sharp special Finsler examples with extreme closed-geodesic/action-separation behavior, not as a disproof.
Katok/Zermelo wind metrics are special near-boundary Randers examples used to separate actions and simplify the computation.

examples.name: Two disks and a ribbon in skein excision
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker uses an excision decomposition by two disks meeting along a ribbon to illustrate what a derived HOMFLY skein-algebra computation for S^2 might look like.
examples.engagement_reason: The two disks and ribbon in skein excision are described as a possible decomposition for computing skein categories, but no actual computation is carried out.

passes.reason: Two disks glued through a ribbon are contractible/basic excision pieces with much less topology than a general surface.
two disks glued along a ribbon are elementary contractible/local excision pieces forming a minimal base-case decomposition
Two disks and a ribbon are contractible elementary excision pieces with minimal surface topology.
Two disks glued along a ribbon are elementary minimal-topology building blocks in the skein excision discussion.
